feedback.text: As a politically engaged European citizen, & active consumer, I think the Digital Fairness Act (DFA) would be a dependable installation into European legislation to defend consumers from malpractice. However, to make the legislation more encompassing, & a better shield for citizens, there are key points missing: 1. Restrictions on entertainment providers by Payment Processors, namely Mastercard & Visa, using an effective monopoly to restrict entertainment content that this third-party deems "unsavory", even if this said content might not be unlawful. Payment Processors have an inherent control over any system they act as a middleman for, seeing as, in many cases, Payment Processors are the only method of completing a purchase. As such, it would be inherently harmful for consumers' rights to lack any sort of restrictions/regulations on how Payment Processors are allowed to moderate their own services. 2. Unrestricted access, ( & or encouraged), to short form media content, such as YouTube Shorts, TikTok, Instagram(Meta) Reels, Facebook(Meta) reels, SnapChat Stories, etc... It has been proven, (Xie J, Xu X, Zhang Y, Tan Y, Wu D, Shi M, Huang H. The effect of short-form video addiction on undergraduates' academic procrastination: a moderated mediation model. Front Psychol. 2023 Dec 15;14:1298361. doi: 10.3389/fpsyg.2023.1298361. PMID: 38162977; PMCID: PMC10756502.) that short form content has a decisive, negative impact on the human attention span. Social Media Content Providers (SMCP) need regulation for consumers to be given the option to outright disable access to short form content, such as videos under a certain time length, & specifically with a 'scrolling' functionality. Many applications made by SMPCs encourage users to get "sucked in" to a feed of short form content by pushing it in their UI(user-interface), & social feeds. An example of this would be the YouTube opening the 'Shorts' section upon launch. A definition for short form content needs to be established by legislators, & legislation for consumer protection against short form content should be implemented in the DFA. 3. Video Game Publishers (VGPs) discontinuation of video games. VGPs have, in certain cases discontinued their products (eg. "The Crew", Ubisoft) & shutting off access from consumers to play or use the game. Even though consumers made a non-rental purchase, they can no longer access the product. There needs to be encompassing legislation surrounding this deceptive practice. Consumers are being sold 'de-facto' licenses to products, in which the VGP reserves to right to revoke at any time, for any reason, under the guise of a traditional transaction in which the consumer would get to own what they purchased. 4. Restrictions on AI content. Currently there is very little legislation on the use of AI for content production on SMCPs. The DFA should contain requirements for users/consumers to be informed on what content contains AI produces elements, to be given the option to filter out content with AI elements, & to be able to report content that may contain AI elements to both the SMCPs & other users on said platform. Letting SMCPs host content without restrictive regulation on AI content opens the doors for deceptive, & harmful, practices that hurt citizens abilities to perceive information, think critically, & navigate digital environments. (Pomerantsev, P. (2019). This Is Not Propaganda. Faber & Faber.) (Shanmugasundaram, M., & Tamilarasu, A. (2023). The Impact of Digital technology, Social media, and Artificial Intelligence on Cognitive functions: a Review. Frontiers in Cognition, 2(2). https://doi.org/10.3389/fcogn.2023.1203077)
